---
title: "LTE & adaptive timestep"
subtitle: "T4 · Phase T4.2 · `va-transient::integrator`"
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status: code complete — ladder rung 4, formally passed.** Embedded-pair local-truncation-error
control drives accept/reject and step growth/shrink. The diode rectifier formally passes against
real QSPICE golden (2026-07-18, `error=7.925e-4` against a `1e-3` tolerance — the tightest
margin of any passed rung, and a real, unpadded one).
:::

## Goal

Decide, every step, whether the current step size `h` was small enough to trust — and grow or
shrink it accordingly — without a fixed, hand-tuned schedule.

## Where it fits

```
 each step: solve with the primary method AND the "reference" method at the same h
                              │
                    disagreement ──► error ratio ──► accept (maybe grow h) or reject (shrink h)
```

## The idea

**An embedded pair, using the *other* implemented method as the error proxy — not a rigorous
truncation-error estimate.** A textbook higher-order embedded-pair method (RK45's 4th/5th-order
combination, say) needs a genuinely different-order companion built for exactly that purpose.
This project has only two methods implemented, backward Euler and trapezoidal, so
`reference_method` just swaps to *whichever one isn't currently selected* — trapezoidal's own
reference is backward Euler, and vice versa — and their disagreement at the same step size
stands in for the local truncation error. Every accepted step therefore costs roughly 2× a
fixed-step solve (two full Newton solves, not one). The honest tradeoff, stated in this module's
own doc comment: a real divided-difference LTE estimate from several *past* accepted points
would need no second solve per step, but needs a longer history buffer this module doesn't keep
yet — this is the simpler design that's easy to verify directly against an analytic solution,
not the more rigorous one.

**The error ratio is scaled per-unknown, the same `reltol·|x| + abstol` shape Newton's own
convergence check uses** (`t3-core/02-newton.qmd`) — consistent tolerance semantics across the
whole solver, not a separate ad-hoc scheme for the time axis:

```rust
fn lte_error_ratio(x_primary: &[f64], x_reference: &[f64], reltol: f64, abstol: f64) -> f64 {
    x_primary.iter().zip(x_reference).fold(0.0, |worst, (a, b)| {
        let scale = reltol * a.abs() + abstol;
        worst.max((a - b).abs() / scale)
    })
}
```

**Accept/reject/grow/shrink is a small, fixed set of constants**, not a tuned control-theory
loop: `err_ratio <= 1.0` accepts the step; if the ratio is comfortably under `0.5`, the *next*
step grows by `1.5×` (capped at `cfg.tstep`, the caller's own ceiling — this controller only ever
shrinks below it, never grows past it); otherwise the step shrinks by `0.5×` and retries from the
same `t`. A step that would shrink below `cfg.tstep_min` fails with `TimestepUnderflow` rather
than silently accepting an out-of-tolerance result.

## The code

```rust
pub struct TranConfig {
    pub tstart: f64,
    pub tstop: f64,
    pub tstep: f64,       // ceiling; the controller only ever shrinks from here
    pub tstep_min: f64,   // shrinking below this is a hard error, not silent acceptance
    pub method: Method,
    pub lte_reltol: f64,
    pub lte_abstol: f64,  // must be > 0 — the error scale's floor when x is near zero
}
```

Each step attempt:

```rust
let primary = Companion::for_method(cfg.method, &q_prev, &r_prev, step_h, &is_dynamic);
let x_primary = newton_step(instances, dim, &x, &primary)?;
let reference_companion = Companion::for_method(reference_method(cfg.method), ...);
let x_reference = newton_step(instances, dim, &x, &reference_companion)?;

let err_ratio = lte_error_ratio(&x_primary, &x_reference, cfg.lte_reltol, cfg.lte_abstol);
if err_ratio <= 1.0 {
    // accept x_primary, advance t, maybe grow h
} else {
    // shrink h, retry from the same t (or TimestepUnderflow)
}
```

## It works

```console
$ cargo test -p va-transient adaptive
test integrator::tests::adaptive_stepping_grows_the_step_as_the_transient_flattens ... ok
test integrator::tests::tighter_lte_tolerance_takes_more_steps ... ok
test integrator::tests::trapezoidal_is_more_accurate_than_backward_euler_at_the_same_schedule ... ok
```

`adaptive_stepping_grows_the_step_as_the_transient_flattens` is the real proof: an RC charge
changes fastest early on and flattens later, and the controller is checked to actually take
*bigger* accepted steps late in the run than at the start — not just "the code runs without
error." The diode rectifier (`circuits/rectifier.net`), driven through the real netlist pipeline
and compared against genuine QSPICE output, is ladder rung 4:

```console
$ cargo run -q -p xtask -- validate
[xtask]   PASS circuits/rectifier.net: error=7.925e-4 (tol 1e-3)
```

That `7.925e-4` margin is real and unpadded — the tightest of any rung passed so far, closed
only after `t6-integration/03-validation.qmd`'s two SPICE-comparison fixes (a `UIC` cold-start
translation, and excluding both the caller's own unsolved seed sample *and* any golden sample
earlier than this engine's own first real solved time from the comparison).

## Limitations

`GROWTH_FACTOR`/`GROWTH_ERR_THRESHOLD`/`SHRINK_FACTOR` are fixed constants, not exposed as
`TranConfig` knobs — a circuit that would benefit from a different growth/shrink aggressiveness
has no way to ask for one today. The ~2× per-step cost of the embedded-pair approach (§ above)
is a real, accepted tradeoff, not hidden.

## What's next

`t4-transient/03-events.qmd` — forced exact breakpoints, crossing detection, and the ring
oscillator (ladder rung 6).
